summary: Gruber argues that the commonly drawn distinction between Apple's and Google's cloud strategies is fundamentally misunderstood. Both companies use client-side software communicating with cloud servers — Google's just runs in a browser via JavaScript while Apple's runs natively. He contends that Google's web-based approach isn't simpler or less work; if anything it's harder to create good UX in a browser. The real difference is Apple optimizes for user experience quality while Google optimizes for reach. Gruber also pushes back on the idea that web apps have inherent advantages like shared editing, arguing native apps can do the same. His core reframe: Apple's vision of 'access anywhere' means 'iPhone everywhere,' not 'browser everywhere.'
key_points: Web apps like Gmail are client-side JavaScript applications communicating with servers via APIs — they don't eliminate client-side software, they just change where it runs
Apple optimizes for the best possible user experience; Google optimizes for reaching the largest possible audience — neither strategy is objectively superior
Google's web-based approach is arguably harder, not easier, because creating good UX inside a browser is more difficult than in native apps
Shared document editing is not an inherent advantage of web apps — native apps like SubEthaEdit prove it works without a browser
Apple's answer to 'access anywhere' is having a device always in hand (iPhone), not depending on any available web browser
money_quotes: Web apps like Google Docs and Gmail don't somehow obviate the need for client-side software. They just change where the client-side software runs, and what APIs it is written against.
Google is not getting away with less work. If anything, they're doing more work, because it is harder to create good user experiences inside a web browser.
The mistake is to think that what you see in your browser when you type gmail.com is Gmail. It's not. It's a web-based client to Gmail.
Apple's strategy is correct for optimizing the quality of the user experience. Google's strategy is correct for maximizing the number of users for its apps.
Google's frame is the browser window. Apple's frame is the screen. That's what we'll remember about today's keynote ten years from now.
When am I without a device in hand? Never.
Apple's vision for 'access anywhere' is 'iPhone everywhere'.
key_insight: The distinction between web apps and native apps is not about whether client-side software exists, but about where it runs and which tradeoffs — user experience quality versus audience reach — each company chooses to optimize for.
